title: Suxamethonium
content_markdown:
##### Type

Depolarising muscle relaxant [S4]

- **High-risk medication**

##### Presentations

100mg in 2ml – plastic ampoule

##### Actions

Acts like the neurotransmitter acetylcholine at the neuromuscular junction. Persists for a period long enough to exhaust the motor endplate by prolonged depolarisation.

Onset IV: approx 45 seconds 
 Duration IV: 5 – 7 minutes 

#### Uses

To facilitate airway management in selected patients 

#### Adverse Effects

- Bradycardia

- Potassium release

- Increased intraocular and intragastric pressure

- Occasionally, prolonged paralysis

- Has been associated with malignant hyperthermia

#### Contraindications

- Previous reaction to suxamethonium

- Suspected hyperkalaemia

- Use in children

#### Precautions

- Elderly patient

- Neuromuscular disease

- Hypothermic patient

- Fitting patient

- Patient with reversible pathology

Select patients carefully – always have a fallback position!

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding

##### Pregnancy

- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding

- Safe to use

#### Doses

##### Adult

- **ICP: **1.5mg/kg IV 
- Over 30 – 60 seconds
- To a maximum of 150mg.

##### Paediatric

Not used.

#### Suxamethonium 100mg in 2ml

- Suxamethonium (applies to 100mg in 2ml): 
- 40kg = 60 mg (1.2 ml)
- 50kg = 75 mg (1.5 ml)
- 60kg = 90 mg (1.8 ml)
- 70kg = 105 mg (2.1 ml)- 80kg = 120 mg (2.4 ml)- 90kg = 135 mg (2.7 ml)- 100kg = 150 mg (3 ml)- **Maximum dose 150 mg**

#### Special Notes

- To be used only following completion of the ACTAS designated training programme.
- If heart rate less than 50/minute, consider atropine prior to suxamethonium.
- Suxamethonium may cause bradycardia. If patient is bradycardic once ETT is tied in, consider a dose of atropine.
- **Note:** bradycardia may be a result of a head injury and raised ICP – thus, BP will be elevated. In this case there is no requirement for atropine regardless of the degree of bradycardia.
- Suxamethonium must be drawn up into a red barrelled syringe.
